perturbation.original_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below shall be submitted to arbitration in accordance with The State of California Code of Procedure. The written determination of the arbitration shall be final, binding, and conclusive on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below shall be submitted to arbitration; however, the decision is not binding on matters concerning intellectual property claims related to 'THE COMPANIES'.
perturbation.explanation: By making the arbitration non-binding for intellectual property claims, the contract contradicts the usual enforceability of arbitration agreements under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1281.4 generally enforces arbitration agreements by staying related court actions. The modification introduces an exception for IP claims, undermining the enforceability intended by the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281.4 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.4. If a court of competent jurisdiction, whether in this State or not, has ordered arbitration of a controversy which is an issue involved in an action or proceeding pending before a court of this State, the court in which such action or proceeding is pending shall, upon motion of a party to such action or proceeding, stay the action or proceeding until an arbitration is had in accordance with the order to arbitrate or until such earlier time as the court specifies. If an application has been made to a court of competent jurisdiction, whether in this State or not, for an order to arbitrate a controversy which is an issue involved in an action or proceeding pending before a court of this State and such application is undetermined, the court in which such action or proceeding is pending shall, upon motion of a party to such action or proceeding, stay the action or proceeding until the application for an order to arbitrate is determined and, if
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to arbitration for intellectual property claims, which contradicts the standard enforceability of arbitration agreements. CCP 1281.4, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, enforces arbitration agreements by staying related court actions, indicating a legal preference for arbitration. By carving out an exception that makes arbitration non-binding for IP claims, the modification undermines the intended enforceability of arbitration agreements under California law. This misalignment between the contract's terms and the established legal procedure creates a legal contradiction, as the changed text weakens the legally supported arbitration process.

perturbation.original_text: Essentially, the spirit behind this agreement is one of mutual trust and confidence, and one of reliance on each other to do what is fair and equitable.
perturbation.changed_text: Essentially, the spirit behind this agreement is one of mutual trust, but 'THE COMPANIES' maintain the right to unilateral modification without prior notice.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the right to unilateral modification undermines the principle of mutual trust and fairness, potentially conflicting with contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1309 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts.  The modified text suggests that 'THE COMPANIES' can unilaterally change the agreement without regard for the other party's interests, which can be interpreted as a lack of good faith.
perturbation.location: Paragraph discussing the 'spirit' of the agreement
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules [1301 - 1310] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 30. ) 1309. A term providing that one party or that party’s successor in interest may accelerate payment or performance or require collateral or additional collateral “at will” or when the party “deems itself insecure,” or words of similar import, means that the party has power to do so only if that party in good faith believes that the prospect of payment or performance is impaired. The burden of establishing lack of good faith is on the party against which the power has been exercised. (Added by renumbering Section 1208 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 27. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a unilateral modification clause, which directly undermines the original agreement's foundation of mutual trust. The Law Explanation indicates this may be a breach of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Commercial Code § 1309, addresses good faith in contract performance, stating a party can only accelerate or require collateral 'at will' if they 'in good faith believes that the prospect of payment or performance is impaired.' While the modified text doesn't directly address acceleration or collateral, the principle of good faith is still applicable, as a unilateral modification clause can be used to unfairly change the terms without regard to the other party's interests. This deviates from the principle of mutual trust, creating a situation where 'THE COMPANIES' could act without good faith, contradicting the original spirit of the agreement. Therefore, the modified clause creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This is to confirm that each of the named signatories, separately and individually and their associates hereby agree that his/her corporation(s), division(s), employees, agents and/or consultants will not disclose, make contact with or otherwise be involved in any transaction involving that certain proprietary information and/or client(s), merchant(s), customer(s) has been disclosed by the following companies; Consumers On-Line Development Group, Inc., C.O.L.D. Consumer Net Partners C.N.P. Consumer Net Marketplace C.N.M. CNM Network C.N.M.N. SportCenter Partners S.C.P. SportCenter On-Line S.C.O.L. hereinafter referred to as the "THE COMPANIES", which is hereby made a part hereto this agreement, without the express approval of THE COMPANIES.
perturbation.changed_text: This is to confirm that each of the named signatories, separately and individually and their associates hereby agree that his/her corporation(s), division(s), employees, agents and/or consultants will not disclose, make contact with or otherwise be involved in any transaction involving that certain proprietary information and/or client(s), merchant(s), customer(s) has been disclosed by the following companies; Consumers On-Line Development Group, Inc., C.O.L.D. Consumer Net Partners C.N.P. Consumer Net Marketplace C.N.M. CNM Network C.N.M.N. SportCenter Partners S.C.P. SportCenter On-Line S.C.O.L. hereinafter referred to as the "THE COMPANIES", which is hereby made a part hereto this agreement, without the verbal approval of THE COMPANIES.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'express approval' to 'verbal approval' removes the requirement for written consent and contradicts standard legal interpretations of contracts requiring express approval or consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1624, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. While this specific agreement may not fall under the Statute of Frauds, changing 'express approval' to 'verbal approval' diminishes the clarity and enforceability of the agreement by allowing potentially unverifiable verbal agreements to suffice where written ones were previously implied.
perturbation.location: First paragraph, definition of non-circumvention agreement
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period t
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'express approval' to 'verbal approval' creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the enforceability and clarity of the agreement. While the provided law snippet (California Civil Code § 1624) primarily concerns contracts required to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds, the Law Explanation correctly points out that the change diminishes the agreement's enforceability by allowing potentially unverifiable verbal agreements where written ones were previously implied. The original text implies a documented form of approval. This misalignment with the standard legal interpretation of 'express approval' as requiring written documentation creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: It is also understood that a signatory cannot be considered or adjudged to be in violation of this agreement when the violation is involuntary, due to the situations beyond his/her control: examples being acts of GOD and/or civil disturbances.
perturbation.changed_text: It is also understood that a signatory cannot be considered or adjudged to be in violation of this agreement when the violation is involuntary, unless 'THE COMPANIES' determine otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause 'unless 'THE COMPANIES' determine otherwise' gives the companies arbitrary power to decide what constitutes a violation, negating the provision about involuntary violations and contradicting the legal principle of objective determination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. The modification gives 'THE COMPANIES' unilateral and arbitrary power to determine what constitutes a violation of the contract. This potentially makes the clause unconscionable, as it allows for unfair and one-sided enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause that allows 'THE COMPANIES' to unilaterally determine what constitutes a contract violation, effectively negating the original provision that protected signatories from involuntary violations due to circumstances beyond their control (acts of God, civil disturbances). This contradicts California Civil Code § 1670.5, which addresses unconscionable contracts. Snippet 1 states: 'If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract...'. The added clause, granting the company arbitrary power, makes the contract potentially unconscionable because it allows unfair, one-sided enforcement where the company's determination overrides objective circumstances.
